title: Bullous Pemphigoid
oneLiner: Autoimmune subepidermal blistering disease causing tense blisters and intense pruritus, mainly in elderly (>70); needs prompt diagnosis and treatment to avoid infection and debility.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Stevens-Johnson syndrome / Toxic epidermal necrolysis (SJS/TEN)** — widespread target lesions, mucosal involvement (oral, ocular, genital), skin detachment (positive Nikolsky), systemic illness (fever, lymphadenopathy). *Killer finding: >10% body surface area with epidermal detachment + sheet-like loss of skin.*
- **Pemphigus vulgaris** — flaccid blisters that rupture easily, widespread mucosal erosions (oral, genital), positive Nikolsky sign (blister extends with lateral pressure). *Killer finding: painful oral ulceration with skin fragility — needs urgent dermatology/immunosuppression.*
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Intensely pruritic urticarial/eczematous plaques (prodromal phase, often weeks before blisters)
  - Tense, fluid-filled blisters (bullae) on erythematous or normal skin
  - Usually spares mucosa (unlike pemphigus)
  - Common on flexural areas, trunk, limbs
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Large (1–5 cm), tense bullae with clear or haemorrhagic fluid
  - Blisters not easily ruptured (negative Nikolsky — unlike pemphigus)
  - May have eroded areas after blister rupture
  - No mucosal involvement in most cases; if present, mild and late
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely → less likely:**
  - **Pemphigus vulgaris** – flaccid blisters, positive Nikolsky, painful oral/genital erosions (key differentiator)
  - **Linear IgA disease** – tense blisters arranged in a string-of-pearls pattern; may have milder mucosal involvement
  - **Dermatitis herpetiformis** – intensely pruritic vesicles on extensor surfaces (elbows, knees), associated with coeliac disease
  - **Bullous drug eruption** – history of new medication (e.g., furosemide, ACE inhibitors, penicillins); often photodistributed
  - **Erythema multiforme major** – target lesions, mucosal involvement, often triggered by infection (HSV) or drugs
- **Splitter:** presence of oral/genital mucosal erosions + positive Nikolsky strongly favours **pemphigus vulgaris** over bullous pemphigoid.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Nikolsky test: gentle lateral pressure on blister roof — negative (blister does not extend) in bullous pemphigoid; positive in pemphigus/SJS/TEN → urgent dermatology referral
  - Assess % body surface area of blisters/erosions (≥10% = severe)
  - Look for signs of secondary infection (cellulitis, purulent fluid)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, LFT, CRP, ESR (baseline, screen for infection/inflammation)
  - Anti-BP180 and anti-BP230 antibodies (ELISA) – high sensitivity/specificity
  - Anti-desmoglein 1 and 3 (if pemphigus suspected)
- **Imaging:** Not routinely needed unless suspecting secondary infection (e.g., chest X-ray if septic)
- **Special tests:**
  - **Skin biopsy** (punch biopsy of intact blister + perilesional skin) for histology (subepidermal split with eosinophils)
  - **Direct immunofluorescence** (from perilesional skin) – linear IgG and/or C3 deposition along basement membrane → gold standard
  - **Indirect immunofluorescence** (serum) – circulating anti-BMZ antibodies
- **Diagnostic criteria:** Not formal scoring system; diagnosis based on compatible clinical picture + positive direct immunofluorescence + anti-BP180/BP230 antibodies.
- **Never-miss test:** **Skin biopsy for direct immunofluorescence** – omission = missing diagnosis and treating blindly (steroids without confirmation risks missed pemphigus or drug reaction)
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Severe disease (≥10% BSA blisters or rapidly progressive)** – admit for dermatology review, start topical clobetasol propionate 0.05% ointment (30 g daily) to intact skin; consider oral prednisolone 0.5–1 mg/kg/day (max 60 mg) after infection ruled out (check CRP, swabs)
  - **Mild disease (<10% BSA)** – start topical clobetasol propionate 0.05% (15–30 g daily) to blistered areas and surrounding skin; no need for systemic steroids initially
  - Fluid resuscitation if large areas denuded (Hartmann’s or 0.9% saline, rate per burn formula – e.g., Parkland: 4 mL/kg/%BSA/24h, but use clinical judgment)
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Continue topical steroids (clobetasol) to all affected areas
  - If systemic steroids started (prednisolone 40–60 mg daily), add bone protection: alendronic acid 70 mg weekly + calcium/vitamin D (e.g., Adcal D3 one tab BD)
  - Wound care: do not debride intact blisters; leave roof on. Use non-adherent dressings (e.g., Mepitel, Jelonet) if oozing erosions
  - Monitor for secondary infection: swab any pustules; start oral antibiotics if cellulitis (e.g., flucloxacillin 500 mg QDS – check BNF)
  - Pain management: paracetamol 1 g QDS; consider neuropathic agents if pruritus severe (e.g., gabapentin – check local policy)
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg daily (if not bleeding, check weight/renal function)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **Taper steroids slowly** – reduce prednisolone by 5–10 mg every 2–4 weeks, aiming for lowest effective dose (often 5–10 mg daily maintenance)
  - If poor response or steroid toxicity, consider steroid-sparing agents: **doxycycline** 200 mg daily + **nicotinamide** 500 mg TDS (both safe in elderly) – duration ~12 weeks
  - Specialist dermatology follow-up (2–4 weeks initially) for monitoring and dose titration
  - Consider azathioprine (check TPMT), mycophenolate, or rituximab in refractory cases (all specialist-initiated)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** 0.9% saline or Hartmann’s at maintenance rate (e.g., 100 mL/h), adjust if >10% BSA (use burn guidelines)
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily (if CrCl >30; dose adjust if renal impairment)
  - **Diet:** normal, ensure adequate protein/calories (hypercatabolic state); consider NG if >20% BSA
  - **Observations:** 4-hourly NEWS2; wound chart twice daily
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line (topical):** Clobetasol propionate 0.05% ointment – apply 30 g/day to affected areas (max 50 g/day). For limited disease, apply twice daily for 2–4 weeks then taper.
- **First-line (systemic):** Prednisolone 0.5–1 mg/kg/day (max 60 mg) orally for 2–4 weeks, then gradual taper. Use with PPI prophylaxis (e.g., omeprazole 20 mg daily).
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **Doxycycline** 200 mg orally once daily + **nicotinamide** 500 mg orally three times daily – for mild–moderate disease (avoid doxycycline if <12 years/pregnant)
  - **Azathioprine** 1–3 mg/kg/day (check TPMT before starting) – as steroid-sparing
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Prednisolone: avoid if systemic infection unmanaged; reduce in severe hepatic impairment
  - Doxycycline: avoid in pregnancy/breastfeeding; photosensitivity (advise sun protection)
  - Azathioprine: contraindicated with allopurinol (if needed, reduce azathioprine dose by 60–75%)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Prednisolone: no renal adjustment; caution in severe hepatic impairment
  - Doxycycline: no dose adjustment in renal impairment
  - Azathioprine: reduce dose in moderate-to-severe renal impairment (CrCl <30 – aim for lower end of dose range); avoid in severe pre-existing infection
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Starting high-dose systemic steroids without confirming diagnosis** – risks missing pemphigus, drug reaction, or SJS/TEN (which require different treatment); always biopsy before steroids unless severe and dermatology review same day.
  - **Not providing bone protection with long-term steroids** – elderly patients at high fracture risk; omit bisphosphonate = osteoporotic fracture within months of therapy.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Secondary bacterial infection (cellulitis, sepsis) – common cause of morbidity
  - Fluid and electrolyte imbalance if >10% BSA denuded
  - Skin loss leading to hypothermia, pain, poor healing
- **Chronic:**
  - Relapsing-remitting course; up to 50% relapse after stopping treatment
  - Steroid side effects: Cushing’s, diabetes, osteoporosis, hypertension, cataracts, psychosis
  - Disability from itching and blistering (profound impact on quality of life)
sections.Prognosis: - Chronic disease with intermittent flares; high mortality (~10–30% at 1 year in elderly) from infection, debility, and steroid complications rather than disease itself. Spontaneous remission possible but rare. With appropriate treatment, blisters heal in 2–4 weeks, but pruritus may persist.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always perform a Nikolsky test** – a negative test is reassuring; if positive, suspect pemphigus/SJS/TEN → stop, call dermatology.
- **Use topical clobetasol as first-line for limited disease** – avoids systemic toxicity; apply generously (up to 30–50 g/day) – patient often needs a whole tube per week.
- **For widespread disease, start doxycycline + nicotinamide early** – evidence supports non-inferiority to oral prednisolone with fewer side effects in elderly (BLEPS trial).
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not start systemic steroids without a biopsy** unless extensive (>10%) and rapidly progressive – missing pemphigus or drug reaction delays correct treatment.
- **Do not debride intact blisters** – removing roof increases risk of infection and pain; leave intact to heal.
- **Do not forget bone protection** – every patient on prednisolone >7.5 mg daily for >3 months should receive bisphosphonate + calcium/vitamin D.
- **Do not use emollients only** – itch may be dismissed as eczema; in elderly, unexplained pruritus with bullae must be autoimmune workup.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Increasing number of blisters or new blisters despite topical treatment
  - Signs of infection: redness, pus, swelling, fever, increasing pain
  - Difficulty swallowing or new mucosal ulcers (rare but suggests pemphigus)
  - Sudden worsening of skin detachment (>10% BSA)
- **Follow-up:**
  - Dermatology outpatient clinic within 2 weeks for biopsy result review and treatment plan
  - If on systemic steroids, GP follow-up in 2 weeks for BP check, glucose, and bone protection plan
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior/registrar if:**
  - >10% BSA blisters/erosions (consider burn centre referral if >20%)
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or lactate >2 mmol/L (suspected sepsis)
  - New blisters with mucosal involvement (oral, ocular, genital) – rule out pemphigus/SJS
  - GCS drop or hypotension – consider anaphylaxis to treatment (rare) or septic shock
- **Call ICU if:**
  - ≥20% BSA involvement with haemodynamic instability
  - Respiratory distress from fluid loss or sedation
  - Require central venous access and strict fluid balance monitoring

rawMarkdown:
## Bullous Pemphigoid

**One-liner:** Autoimmune subepidermal blistering disease causing tense blisters and intense pruritus, mainly in elderly (>70); needs prompt diagnosis and treatment to avoid infection and debility.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Stevens-Johnson syndrome / Toxic epidermal necrolysis (SJS/TEN)** — widespread target lesions, mucosal involvement (oral, ocular, genital), skin detachment (positive Nikolsky), systemic illness (fever, lymphadenopathy). *Killer finding: >10% body surface area with epidermal detachment + sheet-like loss of skin.*
- **Pemphigus vulgaris** — flaccid blisters that rupture easily, widespread mucosal erosions (oral, genital), positive Nikolsky sign (blister extends with lateral pressure). *Killer finding: painful oral ulceration with skin fragility — needs urgent dermatology/immunosuppression.*

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Intensely pruritic urticarial/eczematous plaques (prodromal phase, often weeks before blisters)
  - Tense, fluid-filled blisters (bullae) on erythematous or normal skin
  - Usually spares mucosa (unlike pemphigus)
  - Common on flexural areas, trunk, limbs
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Large (1–5 cm), tense bullae with clear or haemorrhagic fluid
  - Blisters not easily ruptured (negative Nikolsky — unlike pemphigus)
  - May have eroded areas after blister rupture
  - No mucosal involvement in most cases; if present, mild and late

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely → less likely:**
  - **Pemphigus vulgaris** – flaccid blisters, positive Nikolsky, painful oral/genital erosions (key differentiator)
  - **Linear IgA disease** – tense blisters arranged in a string-of-pearls pattern; may have milder mucosal involvement
  - **Dermatitis herpetiformis** – intensely pruritic vesicles on extensor surfaces (elbows, knees), associated with coeliac disease
  - **Bullous drug eruption** – history of new medication (e.g., furosemide, ACE inhibitors, penicillins); often photodistributed
  - **Erythema multiforme major** – target lesions, mucosal involvement, often triggered by infection (HSV) or drugs
- **Splitter:** presence of oral/genital mucosal erosions + positive Nikolsky strongly favours **pemphigus vulgaris** over bullous pemphigoid.

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Nikolsky test: gentle lateral pressure on blister roof — negative (blister does not extend) in bullous pemphigoid; positive in pemphigus/SJS/TEN → urgent dermatology referral
  - Assess % body surface area of blisters/erosions (≥10% = severe)
  - Look for signs of secondary infection (cellulitis, purulent fluid)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, LFT, CRP, ESR (baseline, screen for infection/inflammation)
  - Anti-BP180 and anti-BP230 antibodies (ELISA) – high sensitivity/specificity
  - Anti-desmoglein 1 and 3 (if pemphigus suspected)
- **Imaging:** Not routinely needed unless suspecting secondary infection (e.g., chest X-ray if septic)
- **Special tests:**
  - **Skin biopsy** (punch biopsy of intact blister + perilesional skin) for histology (subepidermal split with eosinophils)
  - **Direct immunofluorescence** (from perilesional skin) – linear IgG and/or C3 deposition along basement membrane → gold standard
  - **Indirect immunofluorescence** (serum) – circulating anti-BMZ antibodies
- **Diagnostic criteria:** Not formal scoring system; diagnosis based on compatible clinical picture + positive direct immunofluorescence + anti-BP180/BP230 antibodies.
- **Never-miss test:** **Skin biopsy for direct immunofluorescence** – omission = missing diagnosis and treating blindly (steroids without confirmation risks missed pemphigus or drug reaction)

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Severe disease (≥10% BSA blisters or rapidly progressive)** – admit for dermatology review, start topical clobetasol propionate 0.05% ointment (30 g daily) to intact skin; consider oral prednisolone 0.5–1 mg/kg/day (max 60 mg) after infection ruled out (check CRP, swabs)
  - **Mild disease (<10% BSA)** – start topical clobetasol propionate 0.05% (15–30 g daily) to blistered areas and surrounding skin; no need for systemic steroids initially
  - Fluid resuscitation if large areas denuded (Hartmann’s or 0.9% saline, rate per burn formula – e.g., Parkland: 4 mL/kg/%BSA/24h, but use clinical judgment)
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Continue topical steroids (clobetasol) to all affected areas
  - If systemic steroids started (prednisolone 40–60 mg daily), add bone protection: alendronic acid 70 mg weekly + calcium/vitamin D (e.g., Adcal D3 one tab BD)
  - Wound care: do not debride intact blisters; leave roof on. Use non-adherent dressings (e.g., Mepitel, Jelonet) if oozing erosions
  - Monitor for secondary infection: swab any pustules; start oral antibiotics if cellulitis (e.g., flucloxacillin 500 mg QDS – check BNF)
  - Pain management: paracetamol 1 g QDS; consider neuropathic agents if pruritus severe (e.g., gabapentin – check local policy)
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg daily (if not bleeding, check weight/renal function)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **Taper steroids slowly** – reduce prednisolone by 5–10 mg every 2–4 weeks, aiming for lowest effective dose (often 5–10 mg daily maintenance)
  - If poor response or steroid toxicity, consider steroid-sparing agents: **doxycycline** 200 mg daily + **nicotinamide** 500 mg TDS (both safe in elderly) – duration ~12 weeks
  - Specialist dermatology follow-up (2–4 weeks initially) for monitoring and dose titration
  - Consider azathioprine (check TPMT), mycophenolate, or rituximab in refractory cases (all specialist-initiated)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** 0.9% saline or Hartmann’s at maintenance rate (e.g., 100 mL/h), adjust if >10% BSA (use burn guidelines)
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily (if CrCl >30; dose adjust if renal impairment)
  - **Diet:** normal, ensure adequate protein/calories (hypercatabolic state); consider NG if >20% BSA
  - **Observations:** 4-hourly NEWS2; wound chart twice daily

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line (topical):** Clobetasol propionate 0.05% ointment – apply 30 g/day to affected areas (max 50 g/day). For limited disease, apply twice daily for 2–4 weeks then taper.
- **First-line (systemic):** Prednisolone 0.5–1 mg/kg/day (max 60 mg) orally for 2–4 weeks, then gradual taper. Use with PPI prophylaxis (e.g., omeprazole 20 mg daily).
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **Doxycycline** 200 mg orally once daily + **nicotinamide** 500 mg orally three times daily – for mild–moderate disease (avoid doxycycline if <12 years/pregnant)
  - **Azathioprine** 1–3 mg/kg/day (check TPMT before starting) – as steroid-sparing
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Prednisolone: avoid if systemic infection unmanaged; reduce in severe hepatic impairment
  - Doxycycline: avoid in pregnancy/breastfeeding; photosensitivity (advise sun protection)
  - Azathioprine: contraindicated with allopurinol (if needed, reduce azathioprine dose by 60–75%)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Prednisolone: no renal adjustment; caution in severe hepatic impairment
  - Doxycycline: no dose adjustment in renal impairment
  - Azathioprine: reduce dose in moderate-to-severe renal impairment (CrCl <30 – aim for lower end of dose range); avoid in severe pre-existing infection
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Starting high-dose systemic steroids without confirming diagnosis** – risks missing pemphigus, drug reaction, or SJS/TEN (which require different treatment); always biopsy before steroids unless severe and dermatology review same day.
  - **Not providing bone protection with long-term steroids** – elderly patients at high fracture risk; omit bisphosphonate = osteoporotic fracture within months of therapy.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Secondary bacterial infection (cellulitis, sepsis) – common cause of morbidity
  - Fluid and electrolyte imbalance if >10% BSA denuded
  - Skin loss leading to hypothermia, pain, poor healing
- **Chronic:**
  - Relapsing-remitting course; up to 50% relapse after stopping treatment
  - Steroid side effects: Cushing’s, diabetes, osteoporosis, hypertension, cataracts, psychosis
  - Disability from itching and blistering (profound impact on quality of life)

### Prognosis
- Chronic disease with intermittent flares; high mortality (~10–30% at 1 year in elderly) from infection, debility, and steroid complications rather than disease itself. Spontaneous remission possible but rare. With appropriate treatment, blisters heal in 2–4 weeks, but pruritus may persist.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always perform a Nikolsky test** – a negative test is reassuring; if positive, suspect pemphigus/SJS/TEN → stop, call dermatology.
- **Use topical clobetasol as first-line for limited disease** – avoids systemic toxicity; apply generously (up to 30–50 g/day) – patient often needs a whole tube per week.
- **For widespread disease, start doxycycline + nicotinamide early** – evidence supports non-inferiority to oral prednisolone with fewer side effects in elderly (BLEPS trial).

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not start systemic steroids without a biopsy** unless extensive (>10%) and rapidly progressive – missing pemphigus or drug reaction delays correct treatment.
- **Do not debride intact blisters** – removing roof increases risk of infection and pain; leave intact to heal.
- **Do not forget bone protection** – every patient on prednisolone >7.5 mg daily for >3 months should receive bisphosphonate + calcium/vitamin D.
- **Do not use emollients only** – itch may be dismissed as eczema; in elderly, unexplained pruritus with bullae must be autoimmune workup.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Increasing number of blisters or new blisters despite topical treatment
  - Signs of infection: redness, pus, swelling, fever, increasing pain
  - Difficulty swallowing or new mucosal ulcers (rare but suggests pemphigus)
  - Sudden worsening of skin detachment (>10% BSA)
- **Follow-up:**
  - Dermatology outpatient clinic within 2 weeks for biopsy result review and treatment plan
  - If on systemic steroids, GP follow-up in 2 weeks for BP check, glucose, and bone protection plan

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior/registrar if:**
  - >10% BSA blisters/erosions (consider burn centre referral if >20%)
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or lactate >2 mmol/L (suspected sepsis)
  - New blisters with mucosal involvement (oral, ocular, genital) – rule out pemphigus/SJS
  - GCS drop or hypotension – consider anaphylaxis to treatment (rare) or septic shock
- **Call ICU if:**
  - ≥20% BSA involvement with haemodynamic instability
  - Respiratory distress from fluid loss or sedation
  - Require central venous access and strict fluid balance monitoring
